_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 3-7 Crashing Adult/Impending Arrest (Revised 05/25/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured. For patients < 14 years refer to Pediatric Crashing Patient/Impending Arrest protocol.
protocol_reference: Michigan Adult Treatment Protocol Section 3-7: Crashing Adult/Impending Arrest — MDHHS Approved 5/25/23, Reviewed 2023
purpose: EMS frequently encounters critically ill patients deteriorating toward cardiac or respiratory arrest. The goal is rapid recognition and goal-directed treatments to stabilize the patient and prevent deterioration. Time thresholds are approximate to provide urgency and prioritization — provider safety and scene conditions may affect timing, which should be documented. For patients < 14 years refer to Pediatric Crashing Patient/Impending Arrest-Treatment Protocol.

sections.title: Criteria
points: INCLUSION: (i) Patient in whom cardiac or respiratory arrest appears imminent; (ii) Patient with provider impression of critical illness including new onset altered mental status, airway compromise or severe respiratory distress/failure, and/or signs and symptoms of shock/poor perfusion
EXCLUSION: Life-threatening trauma that has not been corrected (exsanguination, pneumothorax, etc.) — address life threats per trauma protocols first

sections.title: Critical Actions — Within First 5 Minutes
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 3-7: Crashing Adult — First 5 Minutes
points: AIRWAY: Insert nasopharyngeal or oropharyngeal airway as indicated/tolerated if not following commands (GCS motor < 6) or no response to verbal stimuli per Airway Management-Procedure Protocol

BREATHING — if respiratory failure or distress: sit patient up if tolerated and not contraindicated by suspected spine injury; provide high-flow oxygen per Oxygen Administration-Procedure Protocol; if respirations < 10/min: ventilate by BVM at 15 LPM — two-person two-handed technique is most effective and highly recommended when staffing allows; if respirations > 10 but inadequate: apply CPAP for respiratory distress/hypoxia per CPAP-Procedure Protocol (Specialist skill); respirations may be assisted with BVM in sitting position if patient tolerates; consider PPV by BVM if not following commands or SpO2 < 90%
MONITORING: NIBP (cycle every 3 minutes); SpO2; continuous/waveform ETCO2 (Specialist skill); EKG (Specialist skill)

sections.title: Immediate Actions — Within First 10 Minutes
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 3-7: Crashing Adult — First 10 Minutes
points: CIRCULATION: electrical therapy (cardioversion or pacing) if dysrhythmia is primary cause of shock per Electrical Therapy-Procedure Protocol (Specialist skill)
Emergent IV/IO access per Vascular Access & IV Therapy-Procedure Protocol (Specialist skill)
Administer NS or LR up to 1 liter bolus infused under pressure — monitor for pulmonary edema; if pulmonary edema presents, stop fluids and contact Medical Control (Specialist skill)

Consider push-dose epinephrine per Shock-Treatment Protocol (Specialist skill): prepare epinephrine 10 mcg/mL by adding 1 mL of 1 mg/10 mL epinephrine in 9 mL NS; administer 10–20 mcg (1–2 mL of 10 mcg/mL solution) IV/IO; repeat every 3–5 minutes; titrate to SBP > 90 mmHg

sections.title: Actions Within First 15 Minutes
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 3-7: Crashing Adult — First 15 Minutes
points: Re-assess response to treatments
Repeat fluid bolus up to 2-liter total if indicated (Specialist skill)
If bradycardia: consider atropine 1 mg IV/IO if indicated (Specialist skill)
Consider push-dose epinephrine per Shock-Treatment Protocol while administering second fluid bolus (Specialist skill)

sections.title: Actions Within First 20 Minutes
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 3-7: Crashing Adult — First 20 Minutes
points: Re-assess response to treatments
Continue fluids as indicated (Specialist skill)
Continue vasopressors (push-dose epinephrine) as indicated (Specialist skill)
Contact Medical Control for additional fluids/vasopressors
Insert advanced airway if indicated per Airway Management-Procedure Protocol (Specialist skill)

sections.title: Transport
points: Once critical and immediate actions have been completed: move patient to ambulance for transport; transport may be initiated earlier per provider discretion

Actions should be simultaneous — not in any specific order; as critical actions are performed, transport may be initiated; however, transport should NOT supersede initiation of life-saving intervention
NOTE: Concepts can also be used with Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC)-Treatment Protocol to prioritize key interventions prior to transport of cardiac arrest patients with ROSC

key_drugs.name: Push-Dose Epinephrine (Crashing Adult)
key_drugs.dose: 10–20 mcg (1–2 mL of 10 mcg/mL solution) IV/IO — repeat every 3–5 minutes; titrate to SBP > 90 mmHg
key_drugs.indication: Hemodynamic compromise/shock in critically ill patient approaching arrest
key_drugs.notes: PREPARATION: add 1 mL of 1 mg/10 mL epinephrine to 9 mL NS = 10 mcg/mL solution. Titrate to SBP > 90 mmHg. Refer to Shock-Treatment Protocol.

key_drugs.name: NS or LR IV/IO Fluid Bolus
key_drugs.dose: Up to 1 liter infused under pressure — repeat to 2 L total as indicated
key_drugs.contraindications: Signs of pulmonary edema — stop and contact Medical Control
key_drugs.notes: Infuse under pressure for rapid delivery. Maximum 2 L total. Monitor continuously.

key_drugs.name: Atropine (Bradycardia in Crashing Adult)
key_drugs.indication: Bradycardia as contributing factor to hemodynamic compromise
key_drugs.contraindications: Second-degree Mobitz II or third-degree AV block — consider pacing instead
key_drugs.notes: For bradycardia contributing to shock. Refer to Bradycardia protocol as appropriate.

deterioration_flags: SpO2 < 90% — BVM ventilation; CPAP if respirations > 10 but inadequate; advanced airway if not following commands
Respirations < 10/min — BVM at 15 LPM (two-person two-handed technique preferred)
SBP not responding to fluid bolus — push-dose epinephrine (Specialist skill); contact Medical Control for additional vasopressor guidance
Pulmonary edema developing during fluid resuscitation — stop fluids immediately; contact Medical Control
Cardiac arrest — transition to General Cardiac Arrest protocol
scope_notes: Airway adjuncts (NPA/OPA), BVM ventilation, positioning, oxygen: all levels
CPAP: Specialist skill in base Michigan protocol — EMT scope at MCAs with cpap_bls expansion
Push-dose epinephrine, atropine, electrical therapy: Specialist skill
